item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the closest source (here) shows no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very clever and well-executed idea that I enjoyed a lot. My one quibble would be that the suicide backstory feels a little unearned.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Crosslink
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Okay. So there are a few crosslink ideas I have here. The first is turning the mention of Carter in the note to a in-line link to the MC&D Hub, as the MC&D tag implies that this may be a reference to THE carter. Additionally, SCP-1756 mentions testing a movie altered by SCP-1989, so a link in the containment procedures stating that such crosstesting has been approved would go far. Finally, the effect observed here is very similar to that seen in SCP-2251 and a link from here to there would not be too far out of line. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Outside scheduled experimentation times, SCP-1989 is kept in Storage Containment Unit A-29 at Sector-19. Experimentation may only be performed with prior permission from a member of Level 3 personnel, and the tray must be emptied of all testing materials before returning to storage.

To this:

Outside scheduled experimentation times, SCP-1989 is kept in Storage Containment Unit A-29 at Sector-19. Experimentation may only be performed with prior permission from a member of Level 3 personnel, and the tray must be emptied of all testing materials before returning to storage. Testing of materials altered by SCP-1989 with other SCP objects is to be approved on a case by case basis.

Changing this:

When powered on, inserted with a film disc and connected to a compatible television, SCP-1989 appears to operate normally, playing the portion of the film recorded on the film disc correctly and without incident. However, due to each side of a LaserDisc only containing up to sixty (60) minutes of possible recording space, most feature-length films require the playback of both sides, and sometimes multiple discs. SCP-1989 is a single-sided model player; manual inversion and reinsertion of the disc is required during every complete viewing. When any disc previously inserted into SCP-1989 is removed, inverted, and reinserted, the content of the film is changed: when play resumes, the image on the screen is also inverted, and gravity within the filmed environment adjusts accordingly. Within the first few seconds of playback, any unsecured objects, scenery or characters appear to collide with the new bottom of the scene, whether it be the ceiling or open sky.

To this:

When powered on, inserted with a film disc and connected to a compatible television, SCP-1989 appears to operate normally, playing the portion of the film recorded on the film disc correctly and without incident. However, due to each side of a LaserDisc only containing up to sixty (60) minutes of possible recording space, most feature-length films require the playback of both sides, and sometimes multiple discs. SCP-1989 is a single-sided model player; manual inversion and reinsertion of the disc is required during every complete viewing. When any disc previously inserted into SCP-1989 is removed, inverted, and reinserted, the content of the film is changed: when play resumes, the image on the screen is also inverted, and gravity within the filmed environment adjusts accordingly. Within the first few seconds of playback, any unsecured objects, scenery or characters appear to collide with the new bottom of the scene, whether it be the ceiling or open sky.1

Changing this:

Carter

I hope this gives you a change of perspective. Enjoy, my friend.

Happy birthday,
Derwent

To this:

Carter

I hope this gives you a change of perspective. Enjoy, my friend.

Happy birthday,
Derwent

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Whether this effect and that observed in SCP-2251 are variations of the same phenomena is currently under investigation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great, +1, the sheer quirkiness reminds me of SCP-674, which I also enjoy a lot.

The Star Wars Test made me smirk, but then I saw that the next test was about the Poseidon Adventure and I actually burst out laughing before I'd even read it. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great SCP! Upvoted and congratulations! Adding new films maybe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: every time I see this I love it more. It's frikin brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would not have upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I missed out on this one, and it is superb. Tangentially related story time:

When I was younger, my dad would come home from the bar drunk often. Thankfully, the worst thing he would ever do while drunk was try and convince me to watch some classic movies. The few times I agreed usually ended with him passing out within 10 minutes of starting the film. At one point we had a laser disc player, and he convinced me to watch The Outlaw Josie Wales. For whatever reason, that movie had 4 of those gigantic goddam discs, and after the 2nd one, my dad passed out. For the life of me, I could not figure out how to start up the next disc, and I was the sort of kid who could hook-up his own VCR and Nintendo together at age 5. I can't possibly imagine why they never fully caught on….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because the name of one of the people was Carter.

Now I want to write a tale about the Fourth chief justice of the US Supreme Court and tag it marshall-carter-and-dark.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh no, the horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone apparently not the author or Mackenzie seems to have taged this as marshall-carter-and-dark, depsite their being no certain link to them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is a valid point. Let's just strike it up to Rule of Cool. Maybe the ship alters the AG actively based upon the position of a mercury ball in a tube, or something like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: goddammit I feel silly for not getting that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As far as I see it, the note is reversed too, just for fun.
EDIT: As in ,read lines from lowest upwards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, the discovery notes pushed me to the upvote's direction. This SCP simply NEEDS a reason to exist, and reasoning a horribly awful film critic is a really spot on reason. I love when wrong people get slapped in the face with a rainbow fish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it possible that he didn't kill himself? That it was a murder with a change in perspective?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I'm not entirely a fan, I'm glad this is doing well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In a lot of science fiction where artificial gravity is a thing, it works by something on or just under the surface of the flooring exerting a gravitational field equal to that of Earth. So if the ship turns upside down (relative to its original position, remember that in space the concept of up and down is purely relative)

Yeah, but non-artificial gravity on the Earth is relative too, relative to the center of the Earth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was iffy about this.

Then I saw the addendum about the Poseidon Adventure. Well done, sir.

Upvoted.

Also, I notice that SCP articles from 1980-1989 seem to be higher rated than most other articles… wonder if it's because of the numbers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh man, I remember thinking this was an awesome idea in your sandbox, and seeing the finished product is even more awesome. Good job sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So basically, he's an AWCY who hasn't actually discovered/joined AWCY yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the Star Wars test. The fact that nothing changed made me laugh and the fact that it was not the last test made me happy. Too often the "nothing happens" tests are at the beginning or at the end. Though it makes sense that the scene wouldn't be affected, I didn't expect it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In a lot of science fiction where artificial gravity is a thing, it works by something on or just under the surface of the flooring exerting a gravitational field equal to that of Earth. So if the ship turns upside down (relative to its original position, remember that in space the concept of up and down is purely relative) the only non-negligible gravitational force is still exerted by the floor and therefore the characters would notice no difference.

Another way that this article is awesome - good physics!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of Mr. Carter appropriating one of their "oddities" just to make some obnoxious critic he read in a film journal shut the hell up. He also cackles when he reads the obituary in my headcanon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. There doesn't seem to be a reason for the man to commit suicide, though. At the worst he might be discouraged from continuing his career as a critic; perhaps he might even sell his house and most of his possessions and go searching for the meaning of life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) So why aren't narratives with artificial gravity affected?

2) Since the player's effects are in response to the critic's essay, would the SCP only affect fictional narratives? That is, if you put in a documentary laser disc, would it be unaffected?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What would happen if we put in Titanic? Would the ship float up, and would everyone try and cling to the debris to avoid floating up?

This is awesome in so many ways. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The redactions at the end aren't really necessary, and I'm a little confused on whether Masterson made this or received it, but I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm seriously scrabbling to find things wrong with this. All I can find is the recurrent redactions at the end of each test, as others have pointed. Title the section "excerpts from" and you'd lose nothing.

But really, this is clever and goofy and remarkably well thought-out. Also, am I correct in assuming that Carter is the guy whose name follows Marshall's?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked it in Sandbox, liked it here

Plus, on further reflection Derwent Masterson is the perfect name for a pretentious art critic

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I generally downvote about 90% of articles that have GOI references, because they rarely add anything but silliness to an article. However this is kind of an "if my aunt had balls" type question. "If the author had put something bad in this article, would you have downvoted?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice. Well-executed, interesting idea.

Informal Survey:

Who here would've downvoted because of a hypothetical AWCY? reference?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read that as Derwent sending someone an anomalous gift which he thinks proves his point — that the content and character of a film does not change even when you flip it upside down. And sure enough the film struggles to continue being what it is despite the gravity flip.

Then again, the device was recovered from Derwent's home, so it seems like it was something sent to him by someone else… and the artifact hardly proves Derwent's point, but since his point was so terrible to begin with I like to think of him as a loathsome hipster who hates film so much he writes a weekly film review column just to say films aren't art because they do a good job of conveying a film maker's intent. And loathsome hipsters have a way of creating SCPs that reflect themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was initially rather leery of this, but the collapsibles won me over - +1

I agree the notes seem a bit obtuse: if I'm understanding it correctly, the final note would be significantly more comprehensible as follows:

"I hope this gives you a change of perspective. Enjoy, my friend.

Happy birthday, Derwent

Carter"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "She chose doooooown!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I read this upside down and I enjoyed it just as much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those collapsibles are a bit unnecessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …..good lord. Dance Magic Dance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Go ahead, put in Labyrinth. What harm could it do?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this the particular Carter who does business with Marshall and Dark?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is completely original, and a clever idea. I particularly like how you use outdated technology. Adds to the mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this, it actually made me laugh out loud; this might not the point of SCPs, but this is one of the ones I'd just love to play with… Until it fatally messed up my head or something, as is their wont. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I Like the idea, but the suicide of the original owner does seem cliche, and one thing absolutely jumps out at me.
"Initial inspection revealed internal congruence with other models of the same product line, with one exception: a non-standard disc tray different in shape but not material from the original design."
The wording of that last part is extremely awkward. It also really seems like you are trying really hard to be ominous there; a better way to say it might be "Initial inspection revealed internal congruence with other models of the same product line, with the exception of a non-standard disk tray."

Otherwise +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You might want to change the note, then. As it reads, it sounds like it was sent by Derwent to Carter. Also, the means by which the Foundation acquired this are a little bit odd. There are plenty of SCPs that work fine without a discovery log, and I think that this is one of them. Still, good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure about everything else, but as for the third bit:
Change the perspective of the note, just like the player changes the films. =)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the fact that Star Wars did not change. It tells me that the SCP makes changes of the film narrative itself, and is not based on the fact that it is a piece of fiction. It acts as though the movie was real life, and what would happen if gravity, etc were reversed in that universe.

I wonder what would happen in a nature documentary, or a newscast recorded for this machine? Would the people involved know what was happening, unlike the fictional Poirot and McClain?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not the film critic making a point, it's a mysterious stranger making a point to a bad film critic, and the Foundation would probably keep the note since it's a clue about the origin of the item. But I take your point, I just thought the article would have been a little bland and pointless without some sort of backstory. Thanks for the feedback :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is absolutely lovely, such a simple idea very well presented. At first I thought it was going to be too close to my SCP-981 but this is a very unique concept. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this, particularly the Murder on the Orient Express. I do have a few problems, though. First of all, the use of [REDACTED] at the end of each example seemed superfluous. Second, the note didn't do anything for me, and in fact detracted from the article. Before it was a kind of interestingly weird and inexplicable thing, but now it's just some film critic making a really obvious point. When I first saw the note, I thought that this was an attempt to do one of those awful "literal-application-of-a-concept" Are We Cool Yet? articles, and was on the verge of canceling my up vote. The fact that I didn't is a testament to how much I like this. But seriously, the note/excerpt from a film critic that the Foundation would have no reason to care about is just kind of bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure about the suicide angle, but I really enjoyed this one— the note at the end clinched it. Change of perspective, harhar. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aha. I was going to say something, but reversing the note was clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for pointing out the Die Hard thing, it was a copy-paste thing. I agree with the redacted year, I think I just forgot to take it out.

As for the note, well.. like the films, it's undergone a change of perspective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An interesting, weird idea that's well-written and executed. I'm giving this an upvote, but I have a few nitpicks:

  • I don't like that the last numeral of the year that this was recovered was redacted. There's no reason for it.
  • Die Hard came out in 1988, not 1974.
  • I don't get the note. Who's Carter? It sounds like Derwent Masterson is sending the SCP as a gift to someone named Carter, but it's recovered among Derwent's effects.
  • Also, I don't particularly care for the "dude commits suicide" angle here. It's either implying that the SCP made him lose his mind or something, which feels a bit much, or it's an extraneous red herring.
